1: The summary notice: watthî hammilḥāmāh ʾᵃrûḵāh bên bêt šāʾûl ûḇên bêt dāwiḏ — 'there was a long war between the house of Saul and the house of David.' The noun ʾārûḵ (drawn out, extended) from the root ʾāraḵ (to be long, to lengthen) — same root as the promise of long life in the Decalogue. The theological summary follows: dāwiḏ hōlēḵ wᵉḥāzēq wᵉḇêt šāʾûl hōlᵉḵîm wᵉḏalîm — 'David growing stronger and stronger, while the house of Saul grew weaker and weaker.' The paired participles hōlēḵ wᵉḥāzēq / hōlᵉḵîm wᵉḏalîm use the progressive participle construction (hālaḵ + participle) to indicate ongoing, developing trends — not episodic victories but a continuous trajectory.
2: The son-list at Hebron begins: wayyiwwālᵉḏû lᵉḏāwiḏ bānîm bᵉḥeḇrôn — 'sons were born to David at Hebron.' The verb yālad (niphal passive: were born to him) used for royal sons always signals succession politics. Amnon by Ahinoam of Jezreel is firstborn — his seniority and later crime (ch 13) cast a long shadow over the list.
3: Chileab (also called Daniel in 1 Chr 3:1) by Abigail the Carmelite; Absalom by Maacah daughter of Talmai king of Geshur. The Aramean alliance through Absalom's mother is a significant international detail — Geshur is an Aramean kingdom northeast of the Sea of Galilee. Absalom's foreign royal blood through his mother (bat-talmay melek gᵉšûr) will later influence his political ambitions.
4: Adonijah son of Haggith is fourth; Shephatiah son of Abital is fifth. The list moves through six Hebron sons without pausing on any — the names are given precisely because their birth order will matter in succession contests. Adonijah's later claim to the throne (1 Kgs 1:5) rests on his being fourth-born after the deaths of Amnon, Chileab/Daniel, and Absalom.
5: Ithream by Eglah, 'David's wife' (ʾēšet dāwiḏ). The designation is odd — all the other wives are identified by name or origin, not by the redundant 'wife of David.' Some have seen this as a term of endearment or primary-wife status, but the text gives no further indication. The number six mirrors the standard ancient Near Eastern pattern of listing a king's sons from his first six wives in his primary seat of power.
6: The narrative pivot: wayhî bihyôt hammilḥāmāh bên bêt šāʾûl ûḇên bêt dāwiḏ wᵉʾaḇnēr hāyāh mitḥazzēq bᵉḇêt šāʾûl — 'while the war continued, Abner was consolidating his power within the house of Saul.' The verb mitḥazzēq (hithpael participle of ḥāzaq) means to strengthen oneself, to make oneself powerful — the reflexive form indicates self-aggrandizement. The same root was used in 3:1 of David's increasing strength; now Abner is described as doing the same thing from within Saul's house, which is the problem Ishbosheth perceives.
7: The political dynamite: wᵉlᵉšāʾûl pîlegeš wûšmāh riṣpāh bat-ʾayāh — 'Saul had a concubine named Rizpah daughter of Aiah.' The noun pîlegeš (concubine) designates a secondary wife of lower social status but with full legal standing in the household. In ANE succession practice, possession of the former king's harem signified rightful succession to his throne (cf. 2 Sam 16:21-22 where Absalom takes David's concubines as a statement of kingship). Ishbosheth's charge — māddûaʿ bāʾtā ʾel-pîlegeš ʾāḇî, 'why have you slept with my father's concubine?' — is therefore not a moral complaint but a political accusation of usurpation.
8: Abner's furious response: wayyiḥar lᵉʾaḇnēr mᵉʾōḏ ʿal-diḇrê ʾîšbōšet — the phrase wayyiḥar (his anger burned/flared) is the standard idiom for explosive anger (Gen 4:5-6; 30:2; Exod 4:14). Abner's rhetorical question uses the self-deprecating idiom: hᵃrōʾš keleb ʾānōḵî ʾᵃšer lîhûḏāh — 'am I a dog's head, belonging to Judah?' The phrase rōʾš keleb (dog's head) is an expression of worthlessness or contemptibility — a dog was viewed as a scavenging, unclean animal (cf. 1 Sam 17:43; 24:14). The irony is pointed: a man Abner views as trivial has called him a traitor.
9: Abner's oath: kōh yᵃʿᵃśeh ʾĕlōhîm lᵉʾaḇnēr wᵉḵōh yōsîp lô kî kaʾᵃšer nišbaʿ YHWH lᵉḏāwiḏ kî-ḵēn ʾeʿᵃśeh-llô — 'God do so to me, and more, if I do not do for David what the LORD swore to him.' The formula kōh yᵃʿᵃśeh ʾĕlōhîm + wᵉḵōh yōsîp is a self-imprecatory oath formula: 'may God do this and add to it' (i.e., unspecified punishment falls on me if I break this). The reference to YHWH's oath to David (nišbaʿ YHWH lᵉḏāwiḏ) — there is no explicit earlier oath in the narrative, but 1 Sam 16:12-13 and various prophetic declarations are likely in view. Abner acknowledges YHWH's sovereign choice before he acts on it.
10: The scope of the transfer: lᵉhaʿᵃḇîr hammamlaḵāh mibêt šāʾûl ûlᵉhāqîm ʾet-kiseʾ dāwiḏ ʿal-yiśrāʾēl wᵉʿal-yᵉhûḏāh mîddān wᵉʿaḏ-bᵉʾēr šāḇaʿ — 'to transfer the kingdom from the house of Saul and establish David's throne over Israel and Judah, from Dan to Beersheba.' The formula mîddān wᵉʿaḏ-bᵉʾēr šāḇaʿ (from Dan to Beersheba) is the standard idiom for the full extent of the land — Dan at the northern limit, Beersheba at the southern (Judg 20:1; 1 Sam 3:20; 2 Sam 17:11; 24:2). The double object — 'over Israel AND over Judah' — reiterates the divided political reality that has characterized the narrative since 2:4.
11: Ishbosheth's incapacity: wᵉlōʾ yāḵol ʿôḏ lᵉhāšîḇ ʾet-ʾaḇnēr dāḇār miyyirᵉʾātô ʾōtô — 'he could not say another word to Abner, because he was afraid of him.' The verb yārēʾ (to fear) expresses paralysis before a more powerful figure — the king is literally speechless with fear of his own general. The word miyyirᵉʾātô is the noun yirʾāh in construct with a pronominal suffix: 'from his fear of him.' This one clause encapsulates the entire problem: the king has power in name only.
12: Abner's opening to David: lᵉmî ʾāreṣ — 'to whom does the land belong?' The rhetorical question is Abner's way of conceding the theological and practical reality: YHWH has determined that the land belongs to David. The verb in kᵉrōt-bᵉrîtᵉḵā ʾittî — 'make a treaty with me' — uses kārat bᵉrît (to cut a covenant) as always. Abner offers his hand (wᵉhinnēh yādî ʿimmaḵ, 'my hand is with you') — a gesture of personal pledge and alliance.
13: David's one condition: lōʾ tirʾeh ʾet-pānay kî-ʾim-lᵉpānêḵā tāḇîʾ ʾet-mîḵal bat-šāʾûl — 'you shall not see my face unless you first bring Michal daughter of Saul.' The verb phrase rāʾāh pānîm (to see the face) is the idiom for an audience before a superior. David insists on Michal before receiving Abner — the political logic is clear: Michal's return reconstitutes David's marital connection to the house of Saul, providing a claim of legitimacy through the former king's daughter.
14: David sends a parallel demand directly to Ishbosheth: return Michal, for whom 'I paid the bride-price of one hundred foreskins of the Philistines' (ʾašer ʾēraśtî lî bᵉmēʾāh ʿorlôt pᵉlištîm). The noun môhar (bride-price, used in 1 Sam 18:25) is not used here — instead the payment is recalled by description. The act of paying the bride-price establishes ongoing marital rights that override Saul's reassignment of Michal to Palti/Phaltiel (1 Sam 25:44).
15: Ishbosheth's compliance — wayyišlaḥ ʾîšbōšet wayyiqāḥehā, 'he sent and took her' — is immediate and unilateral. He takes his sister from her husband Phaltiel/Palti son of Laish by executive fiat, without Phaltiel's consent — an act that emphasizes Ishbosheth's authority over his own house even while helpless before Abner in other matters.
16: The most pathos-laden detail in the chapter: wayyēleḵ ʾittāh ʾîšāh hālaḵ ûḇāḵōh ʾaḥᵃrêhā — 'her husband followed her, weeping as he walked behind her.' The participle hālaḵ ûḇāḵōh is the participle + infinitive absolute construction indicating persistent ongoing action: he walked-and-wept, the weeping inseparable from the walking. Abner's curt dismissal — šûḇ lēḵ, 'go back' — ends both Phaltiel's grief and his narrative appearance. The scene is the human cost of the political transaction David and Abner have arranged.
17: Abner's diplomatic groundwork: wᵉḏiḇar ʾaḇnēr hāyāh ʿim-ziqnê yiśrāʾēl lēʾmōr — 'Abner had been in contact with the elders of Israel, saying.' The pluperfect (hāyāh + qal perfect in narrative context) indicates previous action: this had been happening even before the Michal transaction. The elders' stated desire — gam-timol ʾešmōl ʿet dāwiḏ lᵉmelek ʿᵃlênû, 'you have long wanted David to be king over us' — acknowledges that popular sentiment was already with David.
18: Abner cites a YHWH oracle: waYHWH ʾāmar ʾel-dāwiḏ bᵉyad dāwiḏ ʿaḇdî hôšîaʿ ʾet-ʿammî yiśrāʾēl miyyaḏ pᵉlištîm ûmiyyaḏ kol-ʾōyᵉḇêhem — 'by the hand of my servant David I will save my people Israel from the hand of the Philistines and from the hand of all their enemies.' This oracle is not found verbatim in 1 Samuel but reflects the substance of 1 Sam 9:16-17 and 16:12-13. The phrase ʿaḇdî (my servant) is the specific designation of a YHWH-chosen covenant agent — used of Moses, Joshua, and pre-eminently of David (Ps 89:3, 20).
19: The special mention of Benjamin — wᵉḏibber ʾaḇnēr gam bᵉʾoznê ḇinyāmîn, 'Abner also spoke in the hearing of Benjamin' — signals the political sensitivity. Benjamin was Saul's own tribe; securing their assent was the most difficult and most necessary part of any transfer of loyalty. The verb dibbēr bᵉʾoznê (spoke in the ears of) indicates private consultation rather than public address.
20: The feast at Hebron: wayyāḇōʾ ʾaḇnēr ʾel-dāwiḏ ḥeḇrônāh wᵉʾittô ʿeśrîm ʾᵃnāšîm — 'Abner came to David at Hebron with twenty men.' A delegation of twenty is a significant but not a state-level mission. David's making of a feast (wayyaʿaś dāwiḏ lᵉʾaḇnēr ûlᵃʾnāšîm ʾᵃšer ʾittô mišteh) is the formal hospitality establishing a covenant relationship — mišteh (feast/drinking party) functions as the social seal of agreements in the ANE.
21: Abner's final words to David: ʾāqûmāh wᵉʾēlēḵāh wᵉʾeqbeṣāh ʾel-ʾᵃḏōnî hammeleḵ ʾet-kol-yiśrāʾēl wᵉyikrᵉtû ʾitᵉḵā bᵉrît ûmālaḵtā bᵉḵōl ʾᵃšer-tᵃʾawweh napšeḵā — 'let me gather all Israel that they may make a covenant with you and you may reign over all that your heart desires.' The phrase kol ʾᵃšer-tᵃʾawweh napšeḵā (all that your soul/life-force desires) uses nepeš in its meaning of deep desire or appetite — a courtly expression of full submission to the king's will. The departure note — wayyēleḵ bᵉšālôm, 'he went in peace' — is the seal of the agreement, but 'in peace' (šālôm) will be ironic within verses.
22: The dramatic arrival of Joab's force: wᵉhinnēh ʾaḇnēr ʾênennû ʿim-dāwiḏ bᵉḥeḇrôn kî šillᵉḥô wayyēleḵ bᵉšālôm — 'Abner was no longer with David at Hebron, for David had sent him away, and he had gone in peace.' The narrator's emphasis — that Abner left 'in peace' and David had 'sent him away' — establishes David's innocence in what follows. The plunder Joab's men bring back (šālāl rāḇ ʾimmām, 'great plunder with them') shows active raiding continuing alongside the diplomatic process.
23: The report to Joab: wayyaggîḏû lᵉyôʾāḇ lēʾmōr bāʾ ʾaḇnēr ben-nēr ʾel-hammeleḵ. The phrasing places Joab in the position of being informed after the fact — he was not present at the negotiation, not consulted, not included. His exclusion from the peace treaty with Abner is the narrative's implicit explanation for his subsequent action.
24: Joab's direct confrontation: meh ʿāśîtā hinnēh bāʾ ʾaḇnēr ʾēlêḵā lāmmāh-zeh šillᵃḥtô wayyēleḵ hālôḵ — 'what have you done? Abner came to you — why did you send him away? He is gone!' The rhetorical questions carry accusation: the verb hālôḵ after wayyēleḵ is an infinitive absolute used for emphasis — he is gone and gone definitively.
25: Joab's intelligence argument: yāḏaʿtā ʾet-ʾaḇnēr ben-nēr kî lᵉfattôṯᵉḵā bāʾ — 'you know Abner son of Ner — he came to trick you.' The verb pātat (to persuade, entice, seduce) — the same root as Delilah's persuasion of Samson (Judg 14:15; 16:5) — suggests deception through persuasion. Joab's accusation may be genuine intelligence assessment or self-serving rationalization; the narrator presents both without adjudication.
26: Joab's action: wayyišlaḥ malʾāḵîm ʾaḥᵃrê ʾaḇnēr wayyāšiḇû ʾōtô mibôr haśśirāh — 'Joab sent messengers after Abner and brought him back from the well of Sirah.' The well of Sirah is otherwise unknown — likely a cistern north of Hebron. The crucial note: wᵉdāwiḏ lōʾ yāḏaʿ, 'David did not know' — the narrator explicitly exonerates David from knowledge of what Joab is planning.
27: The murder: wayyaṭṭēhû yôʾāḇ ʾel-tôḵ haššaʿar lᵉdabbēr ʾittô baSšŝeḵel — 'Joab took him aside in the gateway, as if to speak privately with him.' The gateway (šaʿar) is the customary public meeting place; taking someone aside there (ʾel-tôḵ haššaʿar) implies a private conference — but it also marks the space of civic/judicial activity being perverted for murder. wayyakkēhû šām haḥōmeš wayyāmot — 'he struck him there in the stomach and he died.' The noun ḥōmeš appears again (cf. 2:23): the identical blow that killed Asahel is now replicated as premeditated murder. The reason is stated — ʿal-dam ʿᵃśāhēl ʾāḥîw, 'for the blood of Asahel his brother' — but blood vengeance law required the killing of a murderer, and Abner's killing of Asahel in battle was legally defensive (2:19-23), not murder.
28: David's formal disavowal: wᵉʾānōḵî ûmamlᵃḵtî nᵉqiyyîm mēYHWH ʿad-ʿôlām middǝmê ʾaḇnēr ben-nēr — 'I and my kingdom are forever innocent before the LORD of the blood of Abner son of Ner.' The legal language is precise: nāqî (innocent, clean-handed) is the term used in innocence declarations in judicial contexts (cf. Gen 44:10; Num 5:28). The prepositional phrase mēYHWH (before/from YHWH) establishes the divine tribunal as the reference point — David is declaring his innocence to YHWH, not merely to political observers. The word ʿad-ʿôlām (forever) marks this as a permanent exculpation, not a situational disclaimer.
29: The curse on Joab's house: yāḡūlû ʿal-rōʾš yôʾāḇ wᵉʾel kol-bêt ʾāḇîw — 'let the guilt rest on Joab's head and on all his father's house.' The verb gûl (to be guilty, to bear punishment) in the imperfect jussive calls down curse. The specific afflictions listed — one who has a zāḇ (discharge, bodily flux — possibly gonorrhea or chronic skin disease), one who is leprous, one who falls by the sword, one who lacks bread — are the standard ANE curse catalogue: physical illness, violent death, and famine are the three great threats to life and family continuity. The curse is specific to Joab precisely because David cannot act against him politically at this point (v.39).
30: The narrator's explanatory aside: wᵉyôʾāḇ wᵃʾᵃḇîšay ʾāḥîw hārᵃgû ʾet-ʾaḇnēr ʿal ʾᵃšer hēmît ʾet-ʾᵃśāhēl ʾᵃḥîhem bᵉgiḇʿôn bammilḥāmāh — 'Joab and his brother Abishai killed Abner because he had killed their brother Asahel at Gibeon in the battle.' The narrator is not justifying the murder but explaining its motivation — the blood-vengeance logic that David's curse (v.29) implicitly rejects as an illegitimate extension of gōʾēl rights beyond their legal scope (killing a man in battle is not murder warranting blood-vengeance).
31: David commands public mourning: qirʿû ḇigᵉḏêḵem wᵉḥigru śaqqîm ûsᵉḏû lipnê ʾaḇnēr — 'tear your clothes, put on sackcloth, and mourn before Abner.' The three-part mourning sequence (torn clothing + sackcloth + lamentation) is the standard public grief protocol. Notably, hammelek dāwiḏ hōlēḵ ʾaḥᵃrê hammîṭṭāh — 'King David himself walked behind the bier' — the king as a mourner following the corpse is a striking social inversion of status: the most powerful man walking behind a man he could not protect.
32: The burial at Hebron — Abner's remains interred in the city of David's own reign. The public weeping — wayyibk hammeleḵ ʾel-qeḇer ʾaḇnēr wayyibkû kol-hāʿām, 'the king wept at the grave of Abner, and all the people wept' — is communal mourning that functions politically as well as personally. David's public grief serves to demonstrate that he did not want or engineer Abner's death.
33: The king's lament over Abner: hᵃkᵉmôt nāḇāl yāmût ʾaḇnēr — 'should Abner have died as a fool dies?' The comparison with nāḇāl (the fool) invokes not just the common noun but the specific person Nabal of 1 Sam 25 — whose name means 'fool' and whose death was sudden, unlamented, and without honor. David is saying: Abner deserved better than a fool's death (death by treachery, without warning, in a place of supposed peace). The implicit contrast: a hero deserves to die in battle, not by a hired killing in a city gateway.
34: The elegy continues: yādêḵā lōʾ-ʾᵃsûrôt wᵉraglêḵā lōʾ-lᵉneḥuštayim huggāšt — 'your hands were not bound; your feet were not put in chains.' The passive verbs (ʾāsûr = bound; hugaš = brought/placed) establish the pathos: Abner was not a prisoner who could not defend himself. He walked freely into the killing zone, deceived by the appearance of peace. kinnᵉpōl lipnê ḇᵉnê-ʿawlāh nāpāltā — 'you fell as one falls before treacherous men' — uses the noun ʿawlāh (wickedness, injustice) — the death was not battle-death but judicial murder by treacherous men (ḇᵉnê-ʿawlāh, 'sons of iniquity').
35: The people urge David to eat before the day is over: wayyāḇōʾ kol-hāʿām lᵉhaḇrôt ʾet-dāwiḏ leḥem bᵉʿôḏ hayyôm. David's oath — kōh yᵃʿᵃśeh-li ʾĕlōhîm wᵉḵōh yōsîp kî ʾim-lipnê ḇô-hašŝemeš ʾeṭʿam-leḥem ʾô kol-mᵃʾûmāh — uses the same self-imprecatory formula as Abner's oath in v.9. The fasting-until-sunset mirrors David's mourning fast after Saul's death (1:12) — the formal structure of grief enacted through abstinence from food.
36: The political effect of David's public mourning: wayyakkîrû kol-hāʿām wayyîṭaḇ bᵉʿênêhem — 'all the people took notice, and it pleased them.' The verb nāḵar (hifil, to recognize/acknowledge) indicates a conscious assessment: they recognized (evaluated) what David was doing and approved. David's public grief succeeds as both genuine mourning and political communication.
37: The narrator's summary of the political effect: wayyēḏᵉʿû kol-hāʿām wᵉḵol-yiśrāʾēl bayyôm hahûʾ kî lōʾ hāyᵉtāh min-hammeleḵ lᵉhāmît ʾet-ʾaḇnēr ben-nēr — 'so all the people and all Israel understood that day that the king had no part in killing Abner.' The verb yāḏaʿ (to know) in the qal perfect with wayyᵉḏᵉʿû signals cognitive resolution — the people have arrived at a definite conclusion. The legal vocabulary of the disavowal is carried through: lōʾ hāyᵉtāh min-hammeleḵ, 'was not from the king.'
38: David's tribute to Abner: hᵃlōʾ tēḏᵉʿû kî-śar wᵉḡāḏôl nāpal hayyôm hazzeh bᵉyiśrāʾēl — 'do you not know that a commander and a great man has fallen in Israel today?' The word pair śar wᵉḡāḏôl (commander and great man) is a standard Hebrew idiom for high-ranking officials (Jer 5:5; 26:10; Dan 9:6). The rhetorical question hᵃlōʾ tēḏᵉʿû calls for acknowledgment of what is objectively true — David is not asking permission to mourn but demanding recognition of what the nation has lost.
39: David's admission of weakness: wᵉʾānōḵî hayyôm raḵ wᵉmāšûaḥ melek wᵉhāʾᵃnāšîm hāʾēlleh bᵉnê ṣᵉrûyāh qāšîm mimmennî — 'I am today weak, though anointed king; and these men, the sons of Zeruiah, are too strong for me.' The adjective raḵ (soft, tender, weak) is juxtaposed with māšûaḥ melek (anointed king) — David has the title and the divine anointing, but not yet the political power to control his own military commanders. The verb-less clause qāšîm mimmennî (too hard/strong for me) is a concise admission of political impotence. David concludes with the same appeal to divine justice as the lament's ending: yᵉšallēm YHWH lāʿōśeh hārāʿāh — 'may the LORD repay the evildoer according to his evil.' The verb šālēm (piel: to repay, to requite, to make complete) is the root of šālôm — but here in its retributive meaning: YHWH completes justice by requiting the wrong.
